proposals.method: We will employ gradient-based optimal control theory (e.g., GRAPE or Krotov algorithms) with a cost functional combining the Kibble-Zurek scaling for defect density and a measure of quantum discord (e.g., geometric discord) evaluated on the final system-environment state. The protocol will be simulated for a binary Bose-Einstein condensate model, a system familiar from Zurek's recent work, treating one component as the 'system' and the other as a minimal environment. The control landscape will be analyzed to identify conditions for vanishing discord.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel control paradigm that directly links non-equilibrium quantum dynamics with information-theoretic classicality. It will deliver concrete quench protocols for defect-free, decoherence-resilient state preparation, advancing the practical goals of quantum simulation and metrology. Theoretically, it will provide a dynamical extension of Quantum Darwinism, showing how pointer states can be actively selected through controlled evolution.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of optimal control for defect suppression with an information-theoretic cost function (quantum discord) to engineer pointer states is unprecedented. It moves beyond describing the emergence of classicality to actively prescribing dynamics that enforce it.

proposals.title: Quantum Darwinism and the Classicality of Topological Defects: Scaling in Higher-Dimensional Quantum Phase Transitions
proposals.motivation: The Kibble-Zurek mechanism (KZM) successfully predicts the scaling of defect densities (e.g., kinks) with quench rate in one-dimensional quantum phase transitions, linking non-equilibrium dynamics to universal critical exponents. However, the emergence of classical reality for extended topological defects (like vortices and monopoles) in two and three dimensions, and how their formation is imprinted on the environment via Quantum Darwinism, remains unexplored. This study bridges non-equilibrium quantum thermodynamics, critical phenomena, and the quantum-to-classical transition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scaling of defect densities in higher-dimensional quantum Ising models will obey KZM predictions modified by dimensionality and defect topology, and that the redundant information about these defects—their number and distribution—will be broadcast into the environment, establishing their objective classicality.
proposals.method: We will employ time-dependent matrix product state (MPS) algorithms for 2D systems and quantum Monte Carlo methods for 3D systems to simulate finite-rate quenches across the quantum critical point of the transverse-field Ising model. The formation of domain walls (2D) and vortices (3D) will be tracked. We will then couple the system to a multi-qubit environment and use quantum state diffusion or master equation techniques to study the decoherence and information proliferation (Quantum Darwinism) of the defect configuration, measuring the mutual information between environment fragments and the system's defect observables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive test of KZM scaling for topological defects in higher-dimensional quantum models, extending Zurek's seminal work. It will also establish a direct connection between non-equilibrium defect formation and the emergence of objective classical reality through environmental information broadcasting, a novel application of Quantum Darwinism.
proposals.novelty: While KZM scaling in 1D is well-studied, its interplay with Quantum Darwinism for higher-dimensional topological defects is entirely novel. The proposal uniquely combines non-equilibrium critical dynamics with the theory of quantum decoherence and information to explain how extended classical structures emerge from a quantum universe.

proposals.title: Quantum Darwinism of the Annealing Front: Quench Scaling as a Guide for Optimal Quantum Annealing Schedules
proposals.motivation: Quantum annealing aims to find ground states of complex Hamiltonians for computation, but its performance is limited by non-adiabatic transitions and defect formation. The Kibble-Zurek mechanism (KZM), a pre-2026 cornerstone of quench scaling theory, quantitatively predicts defect densities after crossing a phase transition. This proposal posits that the KZM, which describes the system's loss of adiabaticity, can be inverted to design annealing schedules that minimize computational errors, directly linking foundational non-equilibrium quantum dynamics to practical quantum optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: The scaling of excitations generated during a quantum phase transition, as described by the Kibble-Zurek mechanism, provides a universal theoretical framework to derive non-adiabatic optimal annealing schedules that outperform standard protocols by minimizing defect density in the final computational state.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of analytical KZM scaling theory and numerical simulations on model spin systems (e.g., transverse-field Ising model) and Bose-Einstein condensate analogs. The core technical approach is to treat the annealing front as a critical quench process. We will derive the optimal local annealing rate from the KZM power laws (correlation length ξ ∼ ν^(-1) and relaxation time τ ∼ ξ^(z)) to minimize the density of topological defects or domain walls. This derived schedule, v_opt(t) ∼ t^((zν)/(1+zν)), will be implemented and tested against linear and exponential schedules using quantum Monte Carlo and time-dependent density matrix renormalization group (t-DMRG) simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative connection between the universal theory of non-equilibrium phase transitions and the engineering of quantum annealing protocols. It will produce a new class of annealing schedules grounded in physical scaling laws, potentially leading to higher fidelity ground-state preparation. The results will contribute to both quantum information science, by improving annealing algorithms, and foundational physics, by viewing annealing through the lens of critical dynamics and information preservation.
proposals.novelty: While KZM is well-studied for quenches, its deliberate inversion to *design* an annealing schedule for computational benefit is a novel, non-obvious application. Furthermore, analyzing how the 'classical' information about the solution emerges and becomes robust during this optimized annealing process—a Quantum Darwinism perspective on the annealing front—is an entirely new angle.

proposals.title: Einselection and Quantum Darwinism in the Strong-Coupling Regime: A Non-Perturbative Analysis
proposals.motivation: The theory of decoherence and Quantum Darwinism, which explains the emergence of objective classical reality from quantum substrates, has largely been developed under the assumption of weak system-environment coupling. This limits its applicability to many realistic physical settings, such as solid-state qubits strongly coupled to phonon baths, chemical reactions, or biological processes, where interactions are non-perturbative. A framework for strong coupling is essential to validate the universality of einselection and the proliferation of information central to Quantum Darwinism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that even under strong, non-perturbative system-environment interactions, a generalized form of einselection occurs, selecting preferred pointer states, but that the structure of the resulting branching states and the efficiency of information transfer to the environment differ qualitatively from the weak-coupling paradigm.
proposals.method: We will employ the spin-boson model and related impurity models as paradigmatic testbeds, moving beyond the Born-Markov approximation. The technical approach will combine numerical techniques like the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) and Time-Dependent Variational Principle (TDVP) for matrix product states to simulate the full system-environment dynamics. We will analyze the emergence of pointer states via the time-dependent fidelity of system states and quantify Quantum Darwinism by computing the mutual information between system fragments and multiple environment sub-sectors, using techniques from quantum information theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the foundational framework of decoherence theory into the strong-coupling regime, providing concrete criteria for einselection beyond perturbation theory. It will establish how the objectivity of quantum states, as measured by Quantum Darwinism, is modified or preserved when the system and environment are deeply entangled, bridging a significant gap between abstract theory and experimental platforms like superconducting circuits and quantum dots.
proposals.novelty: While strong-coupling effects have been studied in open quantum systems, a systematic investigation of their impact on the *selection* of pointer states and the *redundant encoding* of information—the core of Quantum Darwinism—remains largely unexplored and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Beyond Eigenstate Selection: A Test of Non-Orthogonal Pointer Bases in Quantum Darwinism
proposals.motivation: The quantum-to-classical transition, explained by decoherence and Quantum Darwinism, posits that the environment selects a 'pointer basis' of system states that are stable and can be redundantly copied. Conventionally, this pointer basis is assumed to be the eigenbasis of a simple system observable (e.g., position). This assumption is foundational but largely untested; it restricts the space of possible classical realities emerging from quantum theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the environment can select a stable, redundantly copied pointer basis that is *not* the eigenbasis of any simple system observable, but rather a set of non-orthogonal, or 'overcomplete,' system states defined by their specific correlations with the environment.
proposals.method: We will employ a model of a central spin system interacting with a spin-bath environment. Using established techniques of decoherence theory, we will calculate the evolution of the system-environment density matrix. We will then apply the quantum mutual information and redundancy analysis central to Quantum Darwinism. Crucially, we will engineer system-environment couplings to favor the proliferation of information about a specific set of non-orthogonal system states (e.g., coherent states of a spin), rather than eigenstates. The emergence of objectivity will be quantified by the partial information plots and the existence of a classical plateau.
proposals.contribution: This work will rigorously test a core, implicit assumption in the decoherence program. It will expand the theoretical framework of Quantum Darwinism by identifying the general conditions for pointer basis selection, potentially revealing a richer landscape of 'classical' states. A positive result would demonstrate that the classical world can be described by overcomplete sets, not just orthogonal eigenstates.
proposals.novelty: While previous work investigates *which* observable is selected, we challenge the premise that the selected states must be eigenstates of *any* simple observable, exploring non-orthogonal pointer bases as a fundamental possibility.

proposals.title: Quantum Darwinism in Reverse: System-Induced Decoherence and State Selection in a Mesoscopic Environment
proposals.motivation: The theory of decoherence and Quantum Darwinism, pioneered by Zurek, explains how an environment selects pointer states of a system and proliferates information about them. This framework treats the environment as a passive, large reservoir. A profound, unexplored question is whether this causal arrow can be inverted: can a well-defined quantum system act upon a less classical, mesoscopic environment to decohere it and select *its* preferred states? Investigating this inversion challenges the foundational assumptions of environment-induced superselection and could reveal new principles of quantum-classical interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: A quantum system with a stable pointer basis can, under specific coupling conditions, decohere a mesoscopic environment and select preferred states within it, leading to the emergence of objective classical properties in the environment itself, as verified by multiple observers probing the environment.
proposals.method: We will employ open quantum systems theory, modeling a central spin (the 'system') coupled to a finite ensemble of spins or bosonic modes (the 'environment'). Using master equation and quantum trajectory simulations, we will calculate the reduced density matrix of the environment. We will quantify the system's effect by monitoring the environment's purity, von Neumann entropy, and the emergence of a stable, redundant information architecture in fragments of the environment using the quantum mutual information and classicality witness measures from Quantum Darwinism. Specific simulations will be conducted using QuTiP (Quantum Toolbox in Python) to model dynamics and information-theoretic metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the theoretical conditions for 'reverse decoherence,' expanding the conceptual framework of Quantum Darwinism. It will provide a new paradigm for understanding how quantum systems can imprint classicality onto their surroundings, with potential implications for quantum control, the design of quantum sensors, and foundational studies of emergent classicality in non-equilibrium settings.
proposals.novelty: While decoherence theory posits the environment as the selector, this proposal inverts that causal relationship, asking if a system can dictate the classical reality of its environment—a possibility not systematically examined in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Decoherence and Quantum Darwinism from Non-Separable Initial Conditions
proposals.motivation: The standard framework of decoherence and Quantum Darwinism universally assumes an initially separable (product) state between the system and its environment. This is a significant idealization; in realistic settings, such as the early universe or strongly interacting condensed matter systems, initial correlations and entanglement are inevitable. A rigorous study of how such non-separable initial conditions affect the emergence of classical objectivity is therefore crucial for the foundational completeness of the quantum-to-classical transition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initial system-environment entanglement will modify the timescales and structure of decoherence, and may either suppress or, in specific cases, surprisingly enhance the proliferation of classical information (Quantum Darwinism) into environmental fragments.
proposals.method: We will employ a spin-boson model and a central spin model, but with initial states prepared as entangled or classically correlated mixed states (e.g., Werner states). Using master equation techniques (e.g., Bloch-Redfield) and numerical simulations of quantum trajectories, we will track the time evolution of the system's purity and the mutual information between the system and environmental fragments. A key analysis will involve comparing the Partial Information Diagram (PID) for separable versus non-separable initial conditions to quantify the emergence of objective information.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the theory of Quantum Darwinism to more physically general initial conditions, providing a critical test of its robustness. It will yield concrete criteria for when initial correlations can be neglected and when they fundamentally alter the pathway to classicality, with implications for cosmology and quantum information processing.
proposals.novelty: While decoherence with initial correlations has been studied, its specific impact on the *redundant encoding* of information—the hallmark of Quantum Darwinism—remains largely unexplored. This proposal directly challenges a core, untested assumption of the objectivity framework.

proposals.title: Decoherence, Quantum Darwinism, and the Emergence of Classical Cosmological Perturbations
proposals.motivation: The quantum origin of classical cosmological perturbations, a cornerstone of modern cosmology, remains conceptually incomplete. While inflation generates quantum fluctuations, the precise mechanism for their transition to a classical statistical distribution is often attributed to 'decoherence' without a detailed, system-environment analysis grounded in quantum information theory. A rigorous open quantum systems treatment is needed to identify the cosmological environment, its interaction Hamiltonian, and the resultant pointer states that become objective via Quantum Darwinism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific fields (e.g., short-wavelength metric perturbations or other matter components) act as the dominant environment, decohering the long-wavelength curvature perturbations into a pointer basis of coherent states, and that the proliferation of this information (Quantum Darwinism) can be quantified, explaining the robustness of the observed classical power spectrum.
proposals.method: We will model the primordial universe as an open quantum system, treating the observable curvature perturbation as the 'system' and shorter-wavelength modes as an 'environment'. Using the influence functional and master equation techniques central to decoherence theory, we will compute the decoherence rate and identify the pointer basis. We will then apply the Quantum Darwinism framework—specifically, the partial information plot—to calculate the mutual information between the system and multiple environment fragments, using numerical simulations of the coupled mode equations in an inflationary background (e.g., slow-roll approximation).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, information-theoretic account of the quantum-to-classical transition in cosmology, moving beyond heuristic arguments. It will yield quantitative predictions for decoherence timescales and the redundancy of information stored in the environment, offering a new lens to test fundamental quantum gravitational models via their implications for cosmological objectivity.
proposals.novelty: While decoherence in cosmology has been studied, the explicit application of Zurek's Quantum Darwinism framework to quantify the emergence of objective classicality in the early universe is a novel synthesis, connecting the consensus about reality in quantum mechanics directly to the origin of cosmic structure.

proposals.title: Decoherence and the Emergence of Classicality in a Single-Molecule Quantum System
proposals.motivation: The quantum-to-classical transition, explained by environment-induced decoherence and quantum Darwinism, is typically studied in mesoscopic or macroscopic systems. However, the minimal physical scale at which these mechanisms robustly select a preferred pointer basis remains unclear. Testing these foundational principles in a single, well-isolated molecule interacting with a simple solvent environment would probe the very limits of classicality, bridging foundational quantum theory and molecular-scale quantum information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that even for a single, small quantum system (a molecule), interaction with a structured but minimal environment (a solvent) will lead to the rapid selection of a specific, stable pointer basis, and that redundant information about this selected state will be imprinted on the environment, demonstrating the onset of Quantum Darwinism at the molecular scale.
proposals.method: We will model a single chiral or floppy molecule (e.g., a bistable molecule like ammonia) as the system, with its rotational/vibrational degrees of freedom coupled to a bath of solvent molecules treated explicitly via molecular dynamics. The decoherence dynamics will be simulated using a hybrid approach: the system's reduced density matrix evolution will be computed via non-Markovian quantum state diffusion or a hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) method, with coupling terms derived from the classical solvent trajectories. We will analyze the preferred pointer states and quantify information redundancy in environmental fragments using quantum mutual information and the quantum Chernoff bound to witness objective emergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed simulation of decoherence and Quantum Darwinism in a truly minimal, chemically realistic composite system. It will establish quantitative benchmarks for how classicality emerges from the bottom up, informing the design of molecular qubits and the understanding of quantum effects in chemistry. It directly tests the scalability of Zurek's framework down to its conceivable limit.
proposals.novelty: While decoherence is studied in spin baths and model systems, applying the full Quantum Darwinism analysis—searching for redundant information encoding—to a realistic single-molecule-solvent composite is novel. It shifts the focus from *if* decoherence occurs to *how* objective classicality emerges in the smallest possible non-trivial units of matter.

proposals.title: Quantum Environmental Spectroscopy: Reconstructing the Spectral Density from Decoherence and Quantum Darwinism Signatures
proposals.motivation: Decoherence theory and Quantum Darwinism explain how the environment selects pointer states and proliferates information about them. However, this framework is typically applied with a known environment. In practice, environments are often complex and poorly characterized. An inverse approach, deducing environmental properties from system dynamics, would be a powerful diagnostic tool for quantum systems, from condensed matter to potential quantum sensors.
proposals.hypothesis: The spectral density and coupling structure of an unknown environment can be uniquely reconstructed from the time-dependent decoherence of a probe system and the ensuing branching structure of its quantum Darwinistic information fragments.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage theoretical and numerical framework. First, using a controlled probe (e.g., a qubit or oscillator), we will simulate its decoherence for model environments (e.g., ohmic, sub-ohmic baths). Second, we will employ Bayesian inference and machine learning techniques (specifically, variational autoencoders and Hamiltonian learning algorithms) to solve the inverse problem: mapping the observed decoherence function and the partial information available to hypothetical 'observers' (modeled by fragment states) back to the most probable environmental spectral density and system-environment coupling operator.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a formal protocol for 'environmental spectroscopy,' turning decoherence from a nuisance into a source of information. It will extend Quantum Darwinism from a descriptive framework to an active measurement paradigm, showing how the redundancy of information directly encodes environmental features. This provides a new theoretical tool for characterizing noise in quantum devices and complex materials.
proposals.novelty: While forward decoherence calculations are standard, the systematic inversion of decoherence and Darwinism data to reconstruct detailed environmental properties is largely unexplored. The key novelty is leveraging the *redundancy* and *structure* of information in multiple fragments—a hallmark of Darwinism—as a constraint to solve the otherwise underdetermined inverse problem.

proposals.title: Non-Markovian Einselection: The Emergence of Pointer States from Correlated Environments
proposals.motivation: The theory of decoherence and Quantum Darwinism explains the emergence of classical objectivity through the environment's selection (einselection) of robust pointer states. However, foundational models typically assume a Markovian environment—a large, featureless reservoir with short-lived correlations. Real physical environments, from structured electromagnetic fields to spin baths in solids, exhibit memory effects and strong internal correlations. Understanding whether and how einselection proceeds in such non-Markovian settings is crucial for applying quantum Darwinism to realistic experimental platforms and for a complete foundational understanding of the quantum-to-classical transition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pointer states can still emerge from non-Markovian or strongly correlated environmental interactions, but that their character, selection criteria, and the timescales for information proliferation (Darwinism) will be qualitatively altered by environmental memory and structure.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of analytical and numerical techniques. First, we will develop generalized spin-boson and central-spin models with structured spectral densities and direct inter-environment couplings to introduce non-Markovianity and correlations. We will then use the tools of quantum information theory—specifically, the spectrum of the conditional mutual information and the partial information decomposition—to track the emergence of objectivity in fragments of the environment. Numerical simulations will leverage tensor network methods, particularly Matrix Product States and the Time-Evolving Block Decimation algorithm, to handle the non-Markovian dynamics of the composite system-environment state efficiently.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the framework of einselection and Quantum Darwinism beyond the standard Markovian approximation, providing a more general criterion for pointer state selection. It will yield concrete predictions for experiments in solid-state systems (e.g., NV centers) and trapped ions, where environmental correlations are significant. The results will clarify the robustness of classical reality in a wider range of quantum universes.
proposals.novelty: While non-Markovian effects on decoherence have been studied, their systematic impact on the *selection* of pointer states and the *redundant encoding* of information—the core of Quantum Darwinism—remains largely unexplored. This proposal directly addresses this gap by applying modern quantum information and many-body simulation tools to Zurek's foundational program.

proposals.title: Einselection of Spacetime: Decoherence and Quantum Darwinism in Cosmological Perturbations
proposals.motivation: A central challenge in quantum gravity is explaining how a classical spacetime geometry emerges from a fundamental quantum description. Pre-2026, the decoherence and einselection program has successfully explained the quantum-to-classical transition for matter, but its application to gravity and cosmology remains underdeveloped. This project aims to bridge this gap by treating spacetime degrees of freedom as a 'system' subject to decoherence from matter fields, addressing the foundational question of why the universe appears classical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interaction of primordial gravitational perturbations (the 'system') with a bath of quantum matter fields leads to their rapid decoherence and einselection of a pointer basis corresponding to adiabatic curvature perturbations, and that these selected states proliferate their information into the environment, exhibiting Quantum Darwinism.
proposals.method: We will employ the open quantum systems framework for cosmological perturbations. The technical approach involves: 1) Deriving a master equation for the reduced density matrix of scalar (curvature) and tensor perturbations, treating other fields (e.g., a scalar inflaton or its decay products) as an environment. 2) Identifying the pointer states that remain robust under this interaction, using the predictability sieve criterion. 3) Calculating the quantum mutual information between a fragment of the environment (e.g., a subset of field modes) and the system to test for the objective existence of classical spacetime via Quantum Darwinism. 4) Using numerical simulations of the master equation dynamics for specific inflationary models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, information-theoretic mechanism for the emergence of classical spacetime from quantum gravity, grounded in the established framework of decoherence. It will yield quantitative predictions for the decoherence timescale of cosmological perturbations and identify the observably relevant pointer basis. This directly contributes to understanding the quantum origin of cosmic structure and the boundary between quantum and classical gravity.
proposals.novelty: While decoherence has been discussed in cosmology, a systematic application of the full einselection and Quantum Darwinism program to gravitational degrees of freedom—treating them as the system of interest—is novel. It moves beyond showing mere decoherence to identifying the specific, robust states that constitute 'classical spacetime' and testing their objective existence.

proposals.title: Einselection to Phase Space: A Formal Derivation of Classical Kinematics from Quantum Mechanics
proposals.motivation: Decoherence and Quantum Darwinism explain the emergence of classical objectivity by identifying pointer states and their proliferation. However, a formal derivation of the full structure of classical phase space—its symplectic geometry and Poisson bracket algebra—directly from the quantum Hilbert space via einselection remains an open challenge. Completing this derivation is crucial for a fundamental understanding of the quantum-to-classical transition, bridging the abstract Hilbert space with the concrete arena of classical dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The symplectic structure of classical phase space, including canonical coordinates and momenta, emerges uniquely from the stability criteria of einselection under specific system-environment interactions. The Poisson bracket algebra can be derived from the commutator algebra of the selected pointer observables in the macroscopic limit.
proposals.method: We will employ the formalism of decoherence and einselection for a generic open quantum system. Starting with a general Lindblad master equation, we will solve for the pointer observables that commute with the interaction Hamiltonian and remain stable. Using the quantum-to-classical correspondence, we will map the algebra of these selected observables to a classical manifold. We will then apply techniques from quantum measurement theory and information geometry to demonstrate how the Wigner function of the pointer states, under strong decoherence, converges to a probability distribution on a symplectic manifold, thereby defining the phase space volume and canonical structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete formal derivation of classical phase space kinematics from quantum principles, solidifying the foundation of Quantum Darwinism. It will establish a rigorous mathematical link between the emergence of classical reality and the geometric structure of Hamiltonian mechanics, offering new insights into the origin of classical chaos and integrability from a quantum information perspective.
proposals.novelty: While einselection identifies preferred bases, deriving the full symplectic geometry of phase space—its canonical pairs and Poisson structure—from stability and information redundancy is a novel and non-trivial step beyond existing results on pointer states.

proposals.title: Engineering Quantum Darwinism: An Inverse Design Framework for Environment-Induced Pointer States
proposals.motivation: Decoherence and Quantum Darwinism explain the emergence of classical objectivity via the proliferation of pointer state information into the environment. However, the process is typically analyzed in a forward manner: given a system-environment interaction, the resulting pointer states are derived. The inverse problem—designing an environment to select a pre-specified, physically relevant set of pointer states—remains largely unexplored, yet is crucial for controlling quantum-to-classical transitions in quantum information processing and quantum simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given open quantum system and a target observable, it is possible to algorithmically design (or identify) an environment and its coupling such that the eigenstates of that observable become the uniquely stable, einselected pointer states, and their information is redundantly encoded in the environment.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework combining open quantum systems theory and optimization. The core algorithm will treat the system-environment coupling Hamiltonian as a variable to be optimized. Using a gradient-based or evolutionary algorithm, we will minimize a cost function penalizing the loss of purity for the target pointer states while maximizing it for their superpositions, ensuring their stability. We will also maximize the quantum mutual information between fragments of the engineered environment and the system to enforce Darwinistic information proliferation. Model systems will include qubits and quantum harmonic oscillators coupled to bosonic baths or spin environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic design principle for environments that enforce desired classical behaviors in quantum systems. It will establish a new toolkit for quantum control, directly linking the abstract theory of einselection to practical engineering. The results will offer novel insights into the conditions necessary for Quantum Darwinism and test the robustness of pointer state selection.
proposals.novelty: While forward decoherence is well-studied, the inverse design of environments for prescribed einselection is a novel inversion of the standard problem. It moves beyond characterizing emergent classicality to actively programming it, a step not undertaken in prior foundational work.

proposals.title: Minimal Information-Theoretic Environments for the Emergence of Pointer States
proposals.motivation: Quantum Darwinism posits that classical objectivity emerges via the proliferation of system information into fragments of an environment. However, existing models often rely on large, unstructured environments. Determining the minimal environmental resources—in terms of degrees of freedom, entanglement structure, and information capacity—required for classicality is crucial for understanding the quantum-to-classical transition in realistic, finite systems, such as nascent quantum processors or mesoscopic objects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a finite, structured environment with a minimal number of mutually non-commuting observables interacting with the system can induce the selection of a pointer basis and support the redundant encoding of system information, provided the environment's internal dynamics and coupling are appropriately engineered.
proposals.method: We will employ spin-chain and bosonic mode models as minimal environments. Using exact diagonalization and tensor network (TEBD) simulations, we will quantify classicality via the Quantum Darwinism fingerprint: the partial information plot of mutual information between system and environmental fragments. We will systematically vary environment size, internal coupling topology (e.g., nearest-neighbor vs. all-to-all), and interaction type (e.g., system-environment coupling to non-commuting environment operators) to find the minimal configurations that yield robust pointer states and information redundancy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish fundamental lower bounds on the complexity of an environment needed for classicality, bridging the gap between abstract decoherence theory and practical experimental constraints. It will provide design principles for observing quantum Darwinism in controlled laboratory settings, such as trapped ions or superconducting qubit arrays, where environmental engineering is possible.
proposals.novelty: While previous work studies classical emergence in large environments, our search for a *minimal* environment focuses on the necessary and sufficient conditions, moving beyond the asymptotic limit to address the finite-resource scenario, which is non-trivial given the competing demands of decoherence, redundancy, and environment self-interaction.

proposals.title: Quantum Darwinism and the Fragmentation of Entanglement: Tracking Inter-Mode Correlations in Decohered Multimode Systems
proposals.motivation: Quantum Darwinism posits that the proliferation of redundant information about a system into fragments of its environment is the mechanism for the emergence of objective classical reality. However, the detailed evolution of entanglement *within* the environment's degrees of freedom—specifically between distinct environmental modes—during this decoherence process remains poorly characterized. Understanding this inter-mode entanglement landscape is crucial for a complete information-theoretic account of the quantum-to-classical transition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under generic decoherence, inter-mode entanglement in the environment will be suppressed or structured into a branching form, reflecting the emergence of a stable pointer observable. The redundancy of the system's information will be inversely correlated with the persistence of complex, multipartite entanglement between environmental modes.
proposals.method: We will employ a model of a central qubit system coupled to a multimode bosonic environment (e.g., a photon bath or phonon modes). Using the quantum master equation and quantum trajectories formalism, we will simulate the decoherence dynamics. We will quantify inter-mode entanglement using measures like logarithmic negativity for bipartitions and the entanglement witness for multimode states. The analysis will track these measures as a function of time, coupling strength, and environmental spectral density, comparing them to the growth of mutual information between the system and environmental fragments (Quantum Darwinism plot).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative link between the destruction of system-environment entanglement (decoherence), the creation of system-fragment classical correlation (Darwinism), and the reshaping of intra-environmental quantum correlations. It will refine the criteria for objectivity by showing which entanglement structures in the environment are compatible with the emergence of a consensus reality.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies focus on system-environment or fragment-fragment correlations, this proposal directly targets the dynamics of entanglement *within* the environment's own Hilbert space, a less explored but critical component of the decoherence paradigm. It connects the fragmentation of environmental entanglement to the redundancy of information.

proposals.title: Quantum Darwinism in Continuum Fields: Decoherence, Pointer States, and the Emergence of Classicality
proposals.motivation: The Quantum Darwinism program has successfully explained the emergence of objective classical reality from quantum systems via decoherence and information proliferation to multiple environment fragments. However, its core constructions and proofs of decoherence rely heavily on discrete, finite-dimensional models or collections of discrete modes. A fundamental gap exists in extending this framework to genuine continuum quantum fields, which underpin our most fundamental physical theories, from quantum electrodynamics to inflationary cosmology. Understanding how objectivity emerges for field observables is a critical, unresolved step in connecting quantum foundations with quantum field theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the central tenets of Quantum Darwinism—decoherence into pointer states and the redundant encoding of their information in environment fragments—can be rigorously generalized to continuum fields. Specifically, we predict that local field amplitudes (e.g., field quadratures) will emerge as robust pointer observables, and their information will be redundantly disseminated by the field's own environmental degrees of freedom.
proposals.method: We will employ the tools of algebraic quantum field theory and open quantum systems for fields. The approach will model a system field (e.g., a low-energy sector) interacting with an environment field (e.g., high-energy modes or a separate bath field) via a bilinear coupling (e.g., Unruh-DeWitt type). We will adapt the quantum master equation and Born-Markov techniques to the continuum to derive non-unitary evolution. The core technical challenge is to generalize the quantum Chernoff bound method for distinguishing states—used in finite-dimensional Quantum Darwinism proofs—to the infinite-mode case, likely focusing on Gaussian states and using symplectic methods to analyze information replication in field environment fragments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous framework for applying Quantum Darwinism to continuum quantum fields, establishing conditions under which local field observables become objective. It will bridge quantum foundations and quantum field theory, offering new insights into the quantum-to-classical transition in field-theoretic contexts like early universe cosmology and relativistic quantum information. The technical methods developed will be applicable to analyzing objectivity in quantum simulations with ultra-cold atoms in continuous traps.
proposals.novelty: While decoherence in quantum fields has been studied (e.g., inflationary perturbations), a formal generalization of the complete Quantum Darwinism mechanism—including the derivation of pointer states and proof of redundant information encoding for continuum fields—is novel and non-trivial, moving beyond discrete-mode analogies.

proposals.title: Inverse Quantum Darwinism: Engineering System-Environment Hamiltonians for Targeted Decoherence and Information Structures
proposals.motivation: Decoherence theory and Quantum Darwinism explain how specific system-environment interactions select pointer states and proliferate information about them. However, the forward problem—predicting decoherence from a given Hamiltonian—is well-studied, while the inverse problem is not. The ability to design a Hamiltonian that yields a desired decoherence map or information architecture would be a powerful tool for quantum control, enabling the engineering of robust quantum memories or tailored measurement processes.
proposals.hypothesis: For a broad class of physically motivated, CPTP decoherence maps (or objective information structures), one can construct a system Hamiltonian and a system-environment coupling Hamiltonian that generate the desired dynamics in the weak-coupling or singular-coupling limit.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem using the operator-sum representation of quantum operations and the Nakajima-Zwanzig projection technique. The core approach involves treating the desired decoherence map as a constraint and solving for Hamiltonian parameters via an optimization algorithm (e.g., gradient descent on a parameterized ansatz) that minimizes the distance between the target map and the map generated by simulation of the unitary system-environment evolution. We will initially focus on Gaussian models (e.g., harmonic oscillator baths) and spin systems to derive analytical conditions for existence and uniqueness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for the Hamiltonian engineering of decoherence, directly linking control theory to foundational quantum theory. It will yield constructive methods for designing environments that enforce specific pointer states or information broadcasting patterns, advancing efforts in quantum error suppression and the experimental testing of Quantum Darwinism.
proposals.novelty: While the forward problem of decoherence is classic, inverting it to design Hamiltonians for a prescribed decoherence dynamic or information structure is a largely unexplored and non-trivial control-theoretic challenge within the Quantum Darwinism paradigm.

ideas.title: Finite-Size Scaling of Generalized Quantum Correlations in Non-Equilibrium Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the generation and scaling of generalized quantum correlations—quantified using POVM-based measures like measurement-induced nonlocality or generalized discord—in finite quantum systems undergoing a dynamical quantum phase transition, such as a quantum quench. It analyzes how the finite system size modifies the expected Kibble-Zurek scaling laws for the density of these operational correlation 'defects', moving beyond traditional order parameters. The core novelty is determining whether the more physically relevant POVM-based quantumness exhibits different finite-size corrections and scaling exponents compared to projective-measurement-based quantities, providing a rigorous benchmark for near-term quantum devices aiming to generate and utilize quantum correlations.

ideas.title: Quantum Discord-Optimal Control for Defect-Free State Preparation
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes quantum optimal control and quantum discord theory to design novel quench protocols for many-body quantum systems. The core idea is to use control theory to shape the system's evolution such that the final system-environment state minimizes both topological defect density and quantum discord. By enforcing conditions for vanishing discord, the protocol dynamically selects the pointer basis of the open system, making the prepared state inherently stable against subsequent decoherence. This approach moves beyond defect minimization to achieve state preparation that is simultaneously defect-suppressed and decoherence-resilient, a capability neither parent idea addresses alone.

ideas.title: Applying Statistical Complexity Measures to Discord via Ecological Niche Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology to analyze quantum discord. In community ecology, statistical complexity measures from computational mechanics (like epsilon-machines and predictive information) are used to analyze time-series data of species interactions, distinguishing structured, deterministic niches from random noise. We adapt this by treating the correlations within a bipartite quantum state as analogous to an ecological community. By applying epsilon-machine reconstruction to sequences of local measurement outcomes, we build minimal, optimal predictive models for the observed classical correlations. The statistical complexity of these models—the memory required for prediction—serves as a quantifier. The boundary between classical and quantum discord is then identified by contrasting the complexity of the model built from the actual measurement data with that built from data where only classical correlations are presumed to exist, thereby using tools from computational mechanics to rigorously analyze discord's classical-quantum boundary.

ideas.title: Adapting Evolutionary Stable Strategies to Identify Pointer States via Discord Minimization
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from evolutionary game theory. The analogous problem is identifying Evolutionarily Stable Strategies (ESS)—strategies that are resilient to invasion by alternatives. The solution involves analyzing a payoff landscape to find stable attractors. Adapted to the quantum context, we propose to frame the system-environment interaction as a game where the 'payoff' for a system basis is its robustness, measured by low quantum discord. By defining a dynamical selection process (e.g., an iterative map) that favors discord-minimizing bases, the pointer states emerge as the stable fixed points (the quantum ESS) of this process. This provides a novel operational and information-theoretic principle for pointer state selection: they are the bases an open system dynamically evolves to adopt in order to minimize its quantum discord with the environment.

ideas.title: Adapting Weather Prediction Data Assimilation for Tensor Network Quench Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea proposes a method-transfer from Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) to the problem of simulating long-time non-equilibrium quantum dynamics with tensor networks. In NWP, ensemble Kalman filters assimilate sparse observational data into high-dimensional models to correct forecast drift. The adapted solution is to develop a hybrid quantum-classical simulation protocol for quench dynamics in one-dimensional quantum many-body systems. By periodically interrupting a standard matrix product state time-evolution (e.g., via TEBD) to incorporate limited, experimentally measurable data—such as local particle densities or two-point correlations from a quantum simulator—the numerical simulation's state can be corrected. This data assimilation step mitigates the compounding errors from tensor truncation and time integration, effectively 're-focusing' the simulation on the true physical trajectory and extending the simulation's predictive horizon for observables of interest.

ideas.title: Predict emergent many-body localization using quantum discord as a dynamical order parameter
ideas.description: This research program aims to establish quantum discord as a predictive, operational tool for detecting phase transitions in complex quantum systems. It will develop and validate a framework where the scaling and dynamics of discord, calculated for specific spin chain models, serve as an order parameter for the many-body localization transition. The approach combines analytical techniques for tractable models with large-scale tensor network simulations to map the discord phase diagram. Finally, it will propose concrete experimental protocols for trapped-ion or superconducting qubit platforms to measure the predicted discord signatures, thereby testing its fundamental role in quantum thermalization and localization.

ideas.description: This research moves beyond generic open-system modeling by explicitly engineering the system-environment interaction to probe the resilience of specific quantum resources. It employs a master equation framework with a physically derived dissipator, ensuring the model's mathematical consistency and experimental relevance. The core objective is to identify operational regimes, such as specific spectral densities or engineered dissipation, where a targeted quantum advantage is not merely degraded but can be stabilized or even enhanced. This leads to predictive models for designing quantum protocols that are robust in realistic, non-ideal environments.

ideas.title: Hybrid quantum-classical field theory for far-from-equilibrium quantum matter
ideas.description: This project develops a hybrid computational framework that integrates the stochastic Gross-Pitaevskii equation (sGPE) for high-occupancy modes with a tensor network representation for low-occupancy, strongly correlated modes. The system dynamically partitions the Hilbert space during a quantum quench, allowing the sGPE to handle the bulk condensate dynamics while the tensor network accurately captures the generation of entanglement and the precise nucleation of topological defects. This approach directly overcomes the mean-field limitations of the pure sGPE by providing a first-principles description of quantum correlations in defect formation. The method will be validated against exact solutions in 1D and applied to predict defect statistics in 2D quenches of ultracold quantum gases beyond the classical field approximation.

ideas.title: Machine Learning for Optimal Discord-Based Quantum Protocols
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning for discord calculation with the search for its operational interpretations. A neural network is trained to find optimal local measurement bases that minimize conditional entropy, thereby computing discord, but its objective is extended to simultaneously maximize performance in a candidate physical task (e.g., quantum state merging, remote state preparation, or non-classical communication). The synergy lies in using the protocol's success as a feedback signal to guide the ML optimization, which in turn reveals the precise discord-type correlations the protocol consumes. This creates a powerful tool to both discover new operational roles for discord and to design highly efficient quantum protocols tailored to the discord present in specific experimental states.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Hybrid Network for Interpretable Discord Optimization
ideas.description: This approach combines a neural network with a symbolic regression component to optimize quantum discord. The network explores the measurement space under explicit physical constraints, ensuring all proposed bases are physically realizable. The symbolic component then distills the successful strategies into an interpretable analytical expression for the optimal measurement strategy. This provides both a numerical solution and a theoretical model that reveals the underlying principles of discord minimization for a given class of quantum states.

ideas.title: Testing the Breakdown of Short-Range Universality with Engineered Power-Law Interactions
ideas.description: This research tests a specific hypothesis: that engineering a tunable power-law interaction (J(r) ~ 1/r^α) in a quantum Ising system will cause a detectable deviation from short-range critical exponents once α falls below a threshold value. The work combines theoretical analysis to predict the modified scaling relations for order parameter and correlation length with a detailed blueprint for a quantum simulation experiment using a platform like trapped ions. The experimental protocol is designed to measure the critical point and scaling behavior, providing a direct, falsifiable test of whether the long-range couplings alter the universality class as predicted.

ideas.title: Model-free reinforcement learning of robust quantum quench protocols with real-time defect estimation
ideas.description: This project replaces model-dependent quantum optimal control with a model-free reinforcement learning framework to discover quench protocols that minimize topological or excitation defects. The learning agent proposes control waveforms, which are applied to a quantum simulator (e.g., a programmable quantum annealer or a trapped-ion chain), and the resulting defect density is estimated via direct measurement. By iterating this cycle, the agent learns protocols that are inherently robust to experimental imperfections and unknown Hamiltonian parameters. The final output is a robust, experimentally validated control policy that outperforms both traditional optimal control and analytic quenches under realistic, noisy conditions.

ideas.title: Non-Equilibrium Initial State Quench: A Metabolic Preconditioning Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption that a quantum quench starts from a ground state. Drawing an analogy from systems biology, where cells are metabolically preconditioned before a stress test, we propose to investigate quench dynamics starting from a non-equilibrium initial state. The analogous solution involves creating a controlled, non-standard initial condition (preconditioning) and then tracking the system's response to a major perturbation. Adapted to condensed matter or cold atom physics, this means preparing a system in a dissipatively stabilized or driven non-equilibrium steady state, performing a sudden quench of Hamiltonian parameters, and then probing the subsequent dynamics. The goal is to understand how the memory and specific correlations of the initial non-equilibrium state dictate the relaxation pathway, potentially leading to dynamical phase diagrams that are inaccessible from equilibrium initial conditions.

ideas.title: Use Evolutionary Algorithms to Optimize Quantum Discord Calculation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Computational Fluid Dynamics, where evolutionary algorithms are used to optimize complex aerodynamic shapes in a high-dimensional, computationally expensive parameter space. The analogous problem in quantum information is searching the space of local measurement bases to minimize conditional entropy for discord calculation—a similarly complex and non-convex optimization. The adapted research idea employs an evolutionary algorithm (e.g., a genetic algorithm) to evolve a population of candidate measurement bases. Each candidate is evaluated by a fitness function based on the conditional entropy it produces for a target quantum state. Through iterative selection, crossover, and mutation, the algorithm explores the parameter space globally, efficiently converging on measurement bases that minimize entropy and thus accurately quantify quantum discord, offering a robust alternative to gradient-based or exhaustive search methods.

ideas.title: Adapt Reaction-Diffusion Front Propagation Methods to Inhomogeneous Quantum Quenches
ideas.description: This idea proposes to generalize quantum quench theory to spatially non-uniform protocols by drawing an analogy from chemical physics. The analogous problem is the propagation of a reaction front in a non-equilibrium chemical system like the Belousov-Zhabotinsky reaction, which is solved using reaction-diffusion equations to determine front speed and profile. The adapted research idea is to model the inhomogeneous quantum quench by treating the relevant physical quantity (e.g., order parameter) as a field governed by an effective reaction-diffusion equation, where the 'reaction' term originates from the quenched Hamiltonian parameter. The goal is to derive the dynamics of the propagating transition front, predict its velocity and structure, and characterize the spatially dependent excitation spectrum generated behind the front, adapting the mathematical techniques used for analyzing pattern-forming fronts in chemical systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Optimal Control from Chemical Reaction Dynamics to Quantum Annealing Schedules
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from chemical reaction dynamics, where optimal control theory (OCT) is used to design laser pulses that efficiently drive molecules to a target state. The analogous problem in quantum annealing is finding a time-dependent schedule that drives a quantum system from an initial Hamiltonian ground state to the ground state of a problem Hamiltonian. The adaptation involves using quench scaling theory—which provides universal predictions for defect formation as a function of quench rate—to inform the cost function and constraints of an OCT algorithm. By framing the search for an optimal annealing schedule as an optimal control problem, with scaling laws providing essential bounds and fitness metrics, one can computationally derive schedules that outperform standard linear or heuristic ramps, potentially mitigating diabatic transitions and enhancing computational success probabilities for hard optimization problems.

ideas.title: Inferring Critical Exponents via Defect Tomography Using Seismic Inversion Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to inverse problems from geophysics—specifically seismic tomography—to the context of non-equilibrium phase transitions. In seismology, the properties of Earth's interior are inferred from surface wave measurements. Analogously, here, the 'interior' properties are the critical exponents (ν, z) of an unknown quantum or classical system undergoing a dynamical phase transition via a quench. The 'surface measurements' are the spatial distribution and density of topological defects (e.g., vortices, domain walls) created in the quench, which scale according to the KZM. The adapted solution involves: 1) Constructing a precise forward model based on KZM scaling that maps a set of trial exponents and known quench parameters to a predicted defect density pattern. 2) Using an iterative optimization algorithm (akin to full waveform inversion) to minimize the discrepancy between the predicted and experimentally measured defect patterns. This 'defect tomography' approach provides a general method to extract critical exponents in systems where traditional equilibrium measurements are impossible or difficult, effectively solving the inverse scaling problem.

ideas.title: Hybrid Tensor Network Framework for Long-Time Quench Dynamics via Entanglement-Adaptive Representations
ideas.description: This project develops a hybrid, adaptive tensor network algorithm to overcome the fundamental entanglement barrier in long-time quench simulations. The core innovation is a dynamical representation that switches between a matrix product state and a more efficient, entanglement-optimized ansatz (e.g., a neural quantum state or a projected entangled pair state in a dilated 1D geometry) when entanglement surpasses a practical threshold. This adaptive scheme is managed by a time-dependent variational principle solver coupled with a feedback loop that monitors simulation fidelity. The resulting framework aims to extend accessible simulation timescales for generic one-dimensional quantum quenches far beyond the limits of pure MPS methods, enabling the study of late-time thermalization and hydrodynamics.

ideas.title: Multi-Observable Quench Spectroscopy for Robust Critical Exponent Inference
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea proposes a protocol where a system is subjected to a designed suite of quenches, varying both the quench rate and its spatial inhomogeneity. For each quench, a suite of defect observables is measured, including density, spatial correlation functions, and topological cluster statistics. These multi-dimensional datasets are fed into a Bayesian inference engine that compares them to simulations spanning candidate dynamical universality classes, explicitly accounting for unknown microscopic parameters. This approach transforms an ill-posed inverse problem into a statistically well-constrained one, enabling reliable extraction of critical exponents and identification of the governing universality class.

ideas.title: Universal defect scaling from critical-point divergence of the generalized adiabatic parameter
ideas.description: This research develops a universal framework for defect scaling in nonlinear quenches by linking protocol design to the divergence of the adiabatic parameter near the critical point. Instead of testing arbitrary nonlinear functions, protocols are parameterized by the behavior of dλ/dt near λ_c, such as |dλ/dt| ~ |λ-λ_c|^zν, where z and ν are critical exponents. This connects the quench rate's nonlinearity directly to the system's critical dynamics, yielding universal scaling laws. The approach enables prediction of optimal quench shapes for defect suppression and provides a unified classification of protocols based on their adiabatic breakdown.

ideas.title: Apply seismic wave propagation methods to holographic quench dynamics
ideas.description: This research adapts seismic wave propagation methods from geophysics to study quantum quenches via holographic duality. In seismology, sudden disturbances (earthquakes) generate waves that propagate through Earth's interior, with surface measurements used to reconstruct subsurface structure. Analogously, quantum quenches in strongly coupled field theories correspond to sudden injections of energy at the boundary of an anti-de Sitter space in gauge-gravity duality. The adapted approach treats the gravitational bulk as an elastic medium where quenches generate 'holographic seismic waves' propagating inward. By applying seismic tomography and waveform inversion techniques to analyze these bulk perturbations, researchers can use boundary field theory data as 'surface measurements' to reconstruct the dynamical formation of black hole horizons and thermalization processes, providing new computational tools for studying far-from-equilibrium dynamics in strongly coupled quantum systems.
